title: Multi-Factor Authentication Policy
content:
Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Policy v2.1 — Aligned with NIST SP 800-63B, DoD Zero Trust Reference Architecture, and CISA MFA Guidance.

1. SCOPE AND APPLICABILITY
1.1 MFA is mandatory for all users accessing enterprise systems, cloud services, VPN, and remote access gateways.
1.2 MFA applies to all employees, contractors, vendors, and third-party users with access to organizational resources.
1.3 Exemptions require written approval from the CISO and must be reviewed quarterly.

2. APPROVED MFA METHODS
2.1 Tier 1 — Phishing-Resistant (Required for privileged access):
  a) FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware security keys (e.g., YubiKey 5 Series)
  b) Platform authenticators with biometric verification (Windows Hello for Business, Apple Touch ID/Face ID)
2.2 Tier 2 — Standard (Acceptable for general user access):
  a) TOTP-based authenticator applications (Microsoft Authenticator, Google Authenticator)
  b) Push notification-based authentication via approved enterprise MDM-managed apps
2.3 Tier 3 — Legacy (Permitted only with compensating controls, sunset by 2026-06-30):
  a) SMS-based one-time passwords — permitted only when Tier 1 and Tier 2 methods are not technically feasible
  b) Email-based one-time passwords — permitted only for non-sensitive systems

3. PROHIBITED MFA METHODS
3.1 SMS OTP as the sole second factor for systems classified as High or Critical impact.
3.2 Security questions (knowledge-based authentication) do not qualify as a valid authentication factor.
3.3 Unmanaged personal devices for push-based authentication without MDM enrollment.

4. ENROLLMENT AND REGISTRATION
4.1 Users must enroll at least two MFA methods to ensure backup access.
4.2 MFA enrollment must occur via an authenticated, in-person or identity-proofed session.
4.3 Self-service MFA reset requires identity verification through the IT Service Desk with photo ID confirmation.
4.4 Recovery codes must be stored in an enterprise-approved password vault, never in plaintext files.

5. SESSION AND RE-AUTHENTICATION
5.1 MFA must be required at every login for High and Critical impact systems.
5.2 For Standard impact systems, MFA may be cached for up to 12 hours on compliant, managed devices.
5.3 Step-up authentication (re-prompting MFA) is required for: accessing admin consoles, modifying security settings, accessing financial or PII data, and performing bulk data exports.
5.4 MFA sessions must be invalidated upon device compliance status change.

6. PRIVILEGED ACCESS
6.1 All administrative and privileged accounts must use Tier 1 (phishing-resistant) MFA.
6.2 Privileged access sessions must require MFA re-authentication every 4 hours.
6.3 Break-glass emergency accounts must use hardware tokens stored in a physical safe with dual-custody access logs.

7. DISABLING MFA
7.1 Disabling MFA for any user or system requires a formal exception request approved by the CISO.
7.2 Temporary MFA bypass for locked-out users may be granted by the IT Service Desk for a maximum of 2 hours, with mandatory re-enrollment within that window.
7.3 All MFA disable/bypass events must be logged and reviewed in the weekly security operations report.
7.4 Unauthorized disabling of MFA is a security incident and must be reported immediately.

8. MONITORING AND COMPLIANCE
8.1 MFA enrollment status must be monitored continuously. Non-compliant accounts must be flagged within 24 hours.
8.2 Failed MFA attempts exceeding 5 in a 10-minute window must trigger an account lockout and SOC alert.
8.3 MFA bypass events must be audited monthly by the security compliance team.

9. REFERENCES
- NIST SP 800-63B Authentication Assurance Levels
- CISA Implementing Phishing-Resistant MFA Fact Sheet
- DoD Zero Trust Reference Architecture v2.0
- CIS Controls v8 Control 6.3, 6.4, 6.5
- Executive Order 14028 Section 3(d)
